Nazi Looted Booksat Penn Libraries
Identify a bookHow they were foundBrowse the booksSources & listsAbout
/about/gaps/
ScopeThe methodThe five stepsWhat we cannot account forRestitution & claims

What we cannot account for

Most institutional registers stop at what they hold. This one keeps a page for what it does not. The numbers below are not failures to hide in a footnote; they are part of the record.

2,391–5,549
The totals never agreed

A JCR distribution document (July 1949–Jan 1952) records 5,549 books sent to Dropsie; Dropsie’s own records from the same years give either 2,391 or 2,591, and no list of the titles received has been found. We hold more than 2,100; the distance to even the low figure is unaccounted for — dispersed, destroyed, or shelved unrecognised.

~430
Lithuanian books with almost no stamp evidence

Of more than four hundred books mostly from Lithuania, only two carry an OAD stamp and none a Nazi stamp. They were never restituted — the United States did not recognise the Soviet annexation of the Baltic states — so their owners must be traced through victim databases rather than marks in the book.

tens of thousands
Records thrown out in a software migration

When the former Dropsie collection moved into Penn’s NOTIS system in the 1990s — which could not display Hebrew — the old Aleph records were discarded, and tens of thousands of books became undiscoverable. Most have since been re-identified in “Find,” but among them was a sizeable number of looted books that surfaced only recently.

16 shelves
Never cataloged, never searched

Sixteen shelves on level B2 — previously uncataloged Hebrew titles — still have no accession numbers and have never been read against the databases. They must be cataloged before any looted books among them can enter the register.

~250–300
“Maybe” cases not yet placed

Books with a faint mark, an ambiguous inscription, or a catalog note but no surviving stamp. Held in a working list, not yet in the register.

6 → 5
Rare manuscripts: six received, five found

A JCR report and the accession list record that Dropsie received six rare manuscripts; five have been located and are in the register. The sixth is unaccounted for.

Where the Baltic books went

The Lithuanian books came a different way. Recovered from the Baltic states but never restituted, they were released to the JCR only in July 1949. The JCR shipped 214 cases of about 29,000 books: 78 cases (12,418 books) to Israel, and 136 cases (16,346 books) to an AJDC warehouse in Paris for a two-year trusteeship, during which it advertised 16,000 owners’ names in the hope of claims. When the trusteeship ended, the remainder was divided 40:40:20 between Israel, the Western Hemisphere, and everywhere else. The books now at the Katz Center are a fragment of that dispersal.

This is a page you can cite. If you are searching for a specific book and do not find it here, its absence may mean it was never sent, or it may mean we have not found it yet. We would rather you know which.

Provenance research & text: Bruce Nielsen · Penn Libraries, Katz Center for Advanced Judaic StudiesUncertainty in this record is shown, not hidden.
